subject: Math

lesson.story_mission: Help the Clock and Coin Crew open the market on time and price each item.
lesson.mini_lesson: Tell time by reading the hour hand and minute hand. Count coins by using each coin value.
lesson.objective: Tell time to five minutes and solve money/counting coin problems.
lesson.concept_explanation: On a clock, the short hand names the hour and the long hand counts minutes by fives. Coin totals come from adding each coin value.
lesson.common_misconception: Learners may confuse the minute hand with the hour hand or count each coin as one.
lesson.real_world_connection: Time and money help plan school days, practices, shopping, and saving.
lesson.lesson_summary: Read the hands or digital time, use context for a.m./p.m., name coin values, and add coins carefully.
hints: Look at the hour hand first.
Count minutes by fives around the clock.
For coins, count from the largest values to the smallest.

worked_examples.text: A clock with the hour hand near 3 and minute hand on 1 shows 3:05.
A movie at 3:05 in the afternoon is 3:05 p.m.
A quarter and a dime and a penny total 36 cents.

hint_ladder: Look at the hour hand first.
Count minutes by fives around the clock.
For coins, count from the largest values to the smallest.

misconception_bank.minute_hand_confusion: Learner may read the minute hand as the hour or count minutes incorrectly.
misconception_bank.hour_hand_between_numbers: Learner may not account for the hour hand moving between numbers as minutes pass.
misconception_bank.coin_value_confusion: Learner may forget penny, nickel, dime, or quarter values.
misconception_bank.coin_counting_skip_error: Learner may skip-count coins in the wrong order or count each coin as one.

level_banks.label: Level 1: Time to 5 Minutes
level_banks.focus: Tell analog clock times to five-minute intervals

questions.prompt: The art center schedule shows an analog clock. What time does it show?
hints: Focus: Notice where the long hand points on this art center schedule.
Strategy: Count by fives from 12 to clock number 1, then read the hour the short hand has passed.
Verify: Check that the minute hand gives 5 minutes and that the short hand is in the matching hour space.

feedback.incorrect: Use the visual model and try again.

questions.explanation: Each clock number represents 5 minutes. The long hand reaches 1, which is 5 minutes after the hour; the short hand has passed 3. Writing the hour and two minute digits gives 3:05.

question_audio.text: The art center schedule shows an analog clock. What time does it show? The visual shows an analog clock with the long minute hand pointing to 1 and the short hour hand a little between 3 and 4
question_audio.label: Read Question

questions.visual_description: an analog clock with the long minute hand pointing to 1 and the short hour hand a little between 3 and 4
questions.accessible_description: an analog clock with the long minute hand pointing to 1 and the short hour hand a little between 3 and 4

level_banks.label: Level 2: A.M. and P.M. / Time Context
level_banks.focus: Match digital times to daily a.m./p.m. contexts

questions.prompt: School starts at 8:05 in the morning. Write the time with a.m. or p.m.
hints: Focus: Find the context word that tells which part of the day this event happens.
Strategy: Keep the shown hour and minutes, then choose a.m. for before noon or p.m. for after noon.
Verify: Read the event again: morning means before noon, so the time label must agree.

feedback.incorrect: Use the visual model and try again.

questions.explanation: The clock gives 8:05. In this context, morning means before noon. Therefore the complete time uses a.m.: 8:05 a.m.

question_audio.text: School starts at 8:05 in the morning. Write the time with a.m. or p.m. The visual shows a digital clock display with 8 as the hour and 05 as the minutes
question_audio.label: Read Question

questions.visual_description: a digital clock display with 8 as the hour and 05 as the minutes
questions.accessible_description: a digital clock display with 8 as the hour and 05 as the minutes

questions.explanation: The clock gives 6:30. In this context, evening means after noon. Therefore the complete time uses p.m.: 6:30 p.m.

level_banks.label: Level 3: Coin Values
level_banks.focus: Name penny, nickel, dime, and quarter values

questions.prompt: Maya finds a penny in her pocket. How many cents is it worth?
hints: Focus: Identify the single penny in this activity.
Strategy: Connect the coin name to its learned value in cents.
Verify: Check the coin-value fact against the picture before answering.

feedback.incorrect: Use the visual model and try again.

questions.explanation: Coin values stay the same even in a new setting. A penny is worth 1 cent, so 1 is the matching response.

question_audio.text: Maya finds a penny in her pocket. How many cents is it worth? The visual shows one penny shown by itself
question_audio.label: Read Question

questions.visual_description: one penny, shown by itself with its denomination label
questions.accessible_description: one penny shown by itself

questions.prompt: A nickel is beside the class store register. What is its value in cents?
hints: Focus: Identify the single nickel in this activity.

questions.prompt: The craft box holds the coins shown. How many cents are there altogether?
hints: Focus: Name each coin in the craft box before adding.
Strategy: Start with quarter worth 25 cents, then add each next coin's value.
Verify: Count the same coins a second way or add the individual values again to confirm the total.

questions.explanation: The coin values are 25, 10, 1 cents. Adding them in the pictured order gives 25 + 10 + 1 = 36, so the collection is worth 36 cents.

level_banks.focus: Mixed time, context, coin value, and money-counting questions

questions.prompt: The community garden opens when the shown clock time arrives. What time is shown?
hints: Focus: Decide whether the event asks you to read a clock, label part of the day, or select a unit.
Strategy: Use the hour and minute information in the garden opening clock; count clock numbers by fives when hands are shown.
Verify: Make sure the response matches both the displayed time and the event words.

feedback.incorrect: Use the visual model and try again.

questions.explanation: The minute information represents 10 minutes after hour 11. Writing the hour followed by two minute digits gives 11:10.

question_audio.text: The community garden opens when the shown clock time arrives. What time is shown? The visual shows an analog clock with the long minute hand pointing to 2 and the short hour hand a little between 11 and 12
question_audio.label: Read Question

questions.prompt: Zuri chooses coins from a change cup for a postcard. How many cents are shown?
hints: Focus: Decide whether this postcard change cup asks for a coin name, a total, or a comparison.
Strategy: Use 10 for the dime, 5 for the nickel, 1 for the penny, 1 for the penny; then add or compare as the prompt requests.
Verify: Recount the pictured coins and check that the answer addresses the final question.

questions.explanation: The pictured values are 10, 5, 1, 1 cents. Their sum is 17 cents, so the answer is 17.

Strategy: Use 25 for the quarter, 25 for the quarter, 5 for the nickel; then add or compare as the prompt requests.
Verify: Recount the pictured coins and check that the answer addresses the final question.

feedback.incorrect: Use the visual model and try again.

questions.explanation: Two quarters make 50 cents, and the nickel adds 5 more. Since 55 is greater than 50, the answer is yes.

questions.prompt: Choir starts at 2:20 p.m. Which unit is best for naming how long one song lasts: minutes or cents?
hints: Focus: Decide whether the event asks you to read a clock, label part of the day, or select a unit.
Strategy: Use the hour and minute information in the choir duration; count clock numbers by fives when hands are shown.
Verify: Make sure the response matches both the displayed time and the event words.

feedback.incorrect: Use the visual model and try again.

questions.explanation: A song lasts for an amount of time. Minutes measure time, while cents measure money, so minutes is the useful unit.

question_audio.text: Choir starts at 2:20 p.m. Which unit is best for naming how long one song lasts: minutes or cents? The visual shows a digital clock with 2 as the hour and 20 as the minutes

questions.prompt: Lena has the shown coins. Can she buy a 30-cent seed packet? Answer yes or no.
hints: Focus: Decide whether this seed packet decision asks for a coin name, a total, or a comparison.
Strategy: Use 25 for the quarter, 5 for the nickel, 1 for the penny; then add or compare as the prompt requests.
Verify: Recount the pictured coins and check that the answer addresses the final question.

feedback.incorrect: Use the visual model and try again.

questions.explanation: The quarter and nickel make 30 cents, and the penny makes 31 cents. Since 31 cents is enough for a 30-cent packet, the answer is yes.

story.text: Listen to this page. You are starting a math mission about Time and Money. Look at the story and think about what the characters need to solve. Math missions are easier when you name the numbers and the model first. Press Start Mission when you are ready.
story.label: Read This Page

lesson.text: This lesson teaches Time and Money. Look at the analog clock and connect each part to the math words. Use the vocabulary on the page to explain your thinking. Remember to check place value, units, or symbols before you answer. Press Next to continue.
lesson.label: Read This Page

watch.text: This page lets you watch a worked example for Time and Money. Follow the model one step at a time. Say what each number means before you look at the final answer. If a symbol appears, read it in words like plus, minus, equals, greater than, or less than. Press Next to continue.
watch.label: Read This Page

demo.text: This demo helps you try Time and Money with support. Build or study the model first, then connect it to the question. A good strategy is to explain the answer in math words before choosing. Press Next when you are ready for practice.
demo.label: Read This Page

practice.text: This is practice. Use the visual model to help you think about Time and Money. Press Read Question if you want the exact question read aloud. Check your strategy before you choose or write an answer. When you are ready, answer the question and keep going.
practice.label: Read This Page

challenge.text: This is a challenge. Use the visual model to help you think about Time and Money. Press Read Question if you want the exact question read aloud. Check your strategy before you choose or write an answer. When you are ready, answer the question and keep going.
challenge.label: Read This Page

checkpoint.text: This is your checkpoint. Use the visual model to help you think about Time and Money. Press Read Question if you want the exact question read aloud. Check your strategy before you choose or write an answer. When you are ready, answer the question and keep going.
checkpoint.label: Read This Page

badge.text: This badge page celebrates your mission work on Time and Money. Look at your stars and remember the strategy you used. Mistakes help show what to practice next. Save your growth data to continue.
badge.label: Read This Page

profile.text: This profile page shows your growth for Time and Money. Look at attempts, correct answers, accuracy, and hints used. Use the recommendation to decide what to practice next. You can continue to Skill Practice or replay the mission.
profile.label: Read This Page
